parts.title: Part 1
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
They started to bloom last week and are already beautiful, but they should peak and be in full bloom at the end of the month. [Q1]

The hills are already covered with orange poppies. [Q2]

From Lakeside, you'll need to drive about an hour east. [Q3]

There are very few people bitten by rattlesnakes, so we do recommend wearing trousers just to be on the safe side. [Q4]

Unfortunately not, since it is a state park, not a national park. [Q5]

We have the general tour, which is the one your friend may have taken, but we also have two more specialised tours. [Q6]

Yes, that is one of the highlights, along with a film introducing the park and the museum. [Q7]

It lasts one hour, and the volunteer will give you information about many of the flowers on the reserve. [Q8]

Yes, so we offer this tour only two times, 5.30 a.m. or 7 p.m. [Q9]

More like a lot of standing, but most of the time you will be at one or two locations, so we recommend bringing a tripod and zoom lens if you want to take pictures. [Q10]

parts.title: Part 2
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Is that too early? [Q11]

As it takes such a long time to cook anything, we end up eating only twice a day. [Q12]

Part 2 You will hear a talk by a volunteer who has been taking part in a project to find out what everyday life was like 2,500 years ago in Britain during the time known as the Iron Age. [Q13]

We have a volunteer who is really good at giving tips on close-up photography. [Q14]

And in Iron Age times, people didn't have shovels or diggers like we do today, but used tools which they fashioned from animal bone. [Q15, Q17]

You mark the centre of your house with a wooden peg. [Q16]

But it's not only this which is relied upon to hold up the roof, so the next thing you have to do is build around the post a timber scaffold tower, similar to the metal ones used in Britain today. [Q18]

Rather like weaving a basket using rods from the willow tree, hazel wood rods are linked to the beams in parallel hoops. [Q19]

First you take reed bundles and cut them so that each is of equal length. [Q20]

parts.title: Part 3
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Well, if I come all that way, I'd like to have a more detailed tour. [Q21]

He argues there'll be plenty of jobs not suitable for machines. [Q22]

Another article was the one looking at how employers will use technology to record what their employees are doing. [Q23, Q29]

Okay, finally, there are the social problems, where night working impacts on the family and social life of the employees. [Q24]

Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30. [Q25]

We need to think about whether they'd serve any real purpose. [Q26]

What about the section on automation? [Q27]

Another thing is the section on workplace structure. [Q28]

And that interview we did with the company manager. [Q30]

parts.title: Part 4
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
I'm sure you're all aware that there has been a huge increase in the number of workers doing regular night shifts in recent years, mainly because of the number of shops and services now open 24 hours, 7 days a week. [Q31]

The first one is what they call the internal clock. [Q32]

And it seems to be generally accepted by all the experts in the field that this clock is linked to not just our behavioural habits, but to cycles of light and dark. [Q33]

The third cause of these problems is the unsocial hours that night workers have to keep. [Q34]

In addition, there seems to be a very high incidence of stomach problems, for example, ulcers among night shift workers. [Q35]

And the most common problem is depression. [Q36]

Secondly, there is a lot of evidence from accident statistics to suggest that mental abilities are badly affected, and that means, for example, decision-making ability and planning ability. [Q37]

And these are what control our performance. [Q38]

The first one is obviously the breakup of family life, and here we can see from the divorce statistics that this occurs more frequently in the case of night workers. [Q39]

But there's also the breakdown of other relationships, not just within the family, but among the peer group. [Q40]
